text: Though I'm sharing a case of a recurrent, recurrent ovarian cancer, this is a twenty something years after original diagnosis. Patients had two laparotomy debulking procedures in the past. She's actively on chemotherapy at this time. and has developed a malignant colovaginal stump fistula and became quite ill from that. Off of chemo for about one month leading up to this, albumin in the toilet, it was a one point one, and a very elderly and firm individual on dialysis.
The goal here was palliation. If we got some oncologic benefit, then that would be great for the patient. It was more the disabling symptoms from the colovaginal fistula.
The surgeon begins by taking down adhesions involving multiple loops of small bowel adhered in the pelvis, primarily using cold scissor dissection to carefully separate the tissues.
So just continue to work your way around. Work where easy. All of this has to get out of the pelvis in order to properly identify the malignant fistula.

The CAT scan showed that there was about a three to four centimeter implant in the left lateral mesorectum. It was not invasive into the colon wall. It was just in the mesorectum. that part was immediately adjacent to where the fistula was.
The surgeon maneuvers a loop of small bowel away from the pelvic area, revealing the colon, fistula, and implant. The previously adhered bowel loops have been over-sewn with Threo silk sutures.
The surgeon identifies the implant located near the internal iliac vessels on the patient's left side, noting its proximity for future surgical steps.
The surgeon begins the colon mobilization using a lateral approach, carefully maneuvering to avoid irreversible steps such as taking the inferior mesenteric artery (IMA).
The surgeon carefully dissects through dense adhesions, gradually working towards the malignant fistula in the pelvis. The tissue appears tightly fused, requiring meticulous separation.
It often doesn't come to a pinpoint. The tumor itself is usually... The malignant fistulas tend to be pretty broad-based and more invasive into tissue around the actual point of where the fistula is.

The surgeon continues to dissect around the area of the fistula, carefully identifying tissue planes. Indocyanine green (ICG) imaging confirms the ureter is not nearby, allowing the surgeon to proceed. Granulation tissue is visible, suggesting proximity to the fistula site.
The surgeon uses a suction irrigator as a retractor to clear smoke and blood from the surgical field, enhancing visibility during dissection.

The surgeon encounters an impasse during dissection, with no visible planes and close proximity to the iliac vessels laterally, near the fistula and the left aspect of the vaginal cuff where the implant is located.

The surgeon attempts to find a more stable dissection plane by moving laterally, aiming to safely navigate around the challenging area. The decision is made to perform a Hartman procedure instead of an anastomosis, focusing on ensuring a safe and definitive operation.
The surgeon identifies a clear dissection plane leading into the pelvis and visually confirms the location of the ureter, ensuring it is not in the path of the dissection.
Kind of bothered me that I was just buzzing there, but fortunately there was no injury. Doing what's easy certainly helps.

The surgeon manipulates the bowel to increase its mobility, exposing the tumor implanted into the lateral mesorectum near the fistula. A small plane is identified around the tumor to facilitate the dissection needed to take down the fistula.

The surgeon navigates the surgical field, initially believing to be in the lateral aspect of the mesorectum but realizing proximity to the iliac vessels. The tumor has invaded beyond the TME plane into the left iliac chain, prompting the surgeon to shift focus to another area due to close proximity to the ureter.

And so a very safe thing to do with these difficult pelvises, and I've presented on this at ASCRS and SAGEs in the past, is go posterior if you hadn't already. And this is going to need to be done in order for me to staple and get the specimen out anyway. So I'm just showing this. I left this case largely unedited, actually, other than camera cleans, mostly just because I wanted you guys to see my struggle.

The surgeon continues a posterior total mesorectal excision (TME) dissection beneath the mesorectum over the sacral promontory, aiming to increase bowel mobility and define the anatomical planes lower in the pelvis.

The surgeon continues the posterior dissection around the right lateral aspect in the TME plane, moving anteriorly to improve anatomical delineation. The dissection progresses directly anteriorly in the rectovaginal septum, staying within the fat plane of the pouch of Douglas, which aids in approaching the area near the metastasis.

The surgeon dissects in the anterior plane, observing the mobility of the bowel and clear yellow fat, likely mesorectum. The dissection is near the vagina, prompting a decision to switch to a posterior approach.

The surgeon joins the left-sided dissection, encountering the tumor that remains adhered to surrounding tissue. The visible plane is identified, and the surgeon considers carving through it to complete the dissection.

The surgeon manipulates lateral tissue, mistakenly pulling it anteriorly while attempting to work in the anterior plane. The ureters are visible as the surgeon navigates through yellow fat, aiming to dissect around the metastatic tissue. The rectovaginal septum is identified, and a vein is noted, which is actually a branch of the left internal iliac vein.

The surgeon encounters difficulty but quickly gains control using a 3-0 Prolene suture to secure a stitch in the tissue. The visual distortion from pulling reveals the invasive nature of the metastasis into the lateral iliac chain tissue, causing the vein to be pulled over. The surgeon initially thought they were working anteriorly but were actually on the left aspect of the iliac chain.

The surgeon uses a vessel sealer to circumferentially dissect the mesorectum distally, enhancing the mobility of the bowel. The fatty plane lateral is visible, and the surgeon carefully navigates around potential iliac bleeding, avoiding the sacrifice of peripheral branches.

The surgeon encounters thickened and edematous tissue, clearly visualizing the junction of the posterior and lateral planes. The vessel sealer is used to carefully dissect along the lateral plane, closely hugging the tumor.

The surgeon carefully dissects around the tumor, aiming to maintain a clean lateral margin. The stitch on the internal iliac vein is visible as the surgeon uses a vessel sealer to precisely separate the tumor from surrounding tissue, avoiding accidental damage to the iliac structures.

The surgeon successfully detaches the metastatic lesion from the pelvic sidewall and proceeds to dissect the distal lateral mesorectum. The ureters are checked and confirmed to be safe, as the surgeon completes the dissection of the lateral mesorectum.

The surgeon cleans the surgical field to prepare for anterior approach and stapling, with visible stitches marking the location of the iliac vessels. The area is clearly distal to both the tumor and the fistula.

The plan for this patient is while diverted to heal, to do some more chemo, and if disease has stabilized and we're able to get the albumin up, I would consider reversing. Obviously, those are always the best laid plans, but we'll distal staple and then finish that off.

The surgeon inspects the pelvis for any bleeding and removes a free-floating piece of tumor. The iliac region is checked and appears satisfactory. A 3-0 Prolene suture is used to mark the rectum for future identification, and the iliac suture is trimmed.
